topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The delivery notice was taped to the door with a flimsy piece of masking tape. It was addressed to 'Tenant B,' a name that felt like a deliberate slight. I stared at the brown cardboard box sitting on my worn welcome mat, the package clearly meant for the person who’d vacated three weeks ago. A familiar tightness constricted my chest, a dull, heavy pressure that made breathing shallow. I didn't want to open it. Every instinct screamed at me to leave it there, an unwelcome artifact of someone else’s life. My gaze drifted to the darkened window, the city lights blurring into streaks of indifferent color. I picked up the box, the cardboard rough beneath my fingertips, and carried it to the corner of the room, setting it down with a soft thud that seemed too loud in the silence.

[story 2]

I found the package tucked behind the recycling bin, an unexpected intrusion into the quiet routine I’d established. The label bore the previous tenant’s name. A slow, cold knot tightened in my stomach as I examined the contents—a collection of old photographs, faded and brittle. My jaw clenched so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. I didn't touch the items immediately. Instead, I paced the small living area three times, the worn floorboards protesting under my heavy steps. The air in the apartment felt thick, stagnant, mirroring the mood settling deep within me. I just wanted the intrusion to vanish, to dissolve back into the anonymity of the past.

[story 3]

The doorbell rang, an abrupt sound that shattered the muted atmosphere of the evening. It was a delivery, and the recipient address was clearly wrong, pointing toward the previous occupant. I felt a deep sense of weariness settle over me, a leaden weight dragging at my shoulders. I retrieved the parcel, its presence an unwelcome reminder of unfinished business. I didn't bother with the formalities of unpacking. Instead, I retreated to the farthest room, sinking onto the edge of the sofa. I stared at the box, tracing the outline of the tape with my eyes, the silence of the apartment amplifying the low thrum of unease vibrating just beneath my skin.
